text: Robotic resection of a gallbladder remnant post laparoscopic cholecystectomy by the Leahy Hospital Group. This was a fifty-seven year old female six years after a laparoscopic cholecystectomy performed for biliary colic. She did well until earlier this year when she had recurrent epigastric pain. LFTs were normal and preoperative imaging revealed remnant gallbladder and cystic duct with multiple stones. Here you can see the coronal cuts of the MRCP showing a gallbladder remnant containing stones.
She was counseled appropriately and taken to the operating room for a robotic resection of her gallbladder remnant.

After establishing pneumoperitoneum, robotic instruments are inserted into the abdominal cavity. The surgeon begins the operation by using robotic tools to take down omental adhesions, separating the omentum from surrounding tissues.

The video shows a bulbous structure identified as the gallbladder remnant, with a tubular structure adjacent to it identified as the common bile duct (CVD). Endocyanin reinjection is used to enhance visualization of these structures.
The video displays the common bile duct, the cystic duct, and the gallbladder remnant, highlighting their anatomical positions and relationships.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to score the peritoneum overlying the gallbladder remnant, with additional scoring performed posteriorly along the infundibulum of the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to safely dissect and expose the insertion of the cystic duct, while scoring the peritoneum anteriorly along the edge of the liver.
The surgeon identifies a tubular structure, likely the cystic duct, using ICG and Firefly technology for enhanced visualization.

The surgeon carefully dissects to further delineate the junction between the cystic duct and the gallbladder remnant, using ICG and Firefly technology to repeatedly check the anatomy and prevent injury to the common bile duct.

The surgeon successfully encircles the cystic duct, confirming its identification with ICG technology. The cystic duct is then transected between robotic plastic clips, completing the separation from the gallbladder remnant.

The surgeon performs a final check before transecting the cystic duct, dissecting it off the liver bed while staying close to the gallbladder edge. The most adherent part is at the fundal end, where old laparoscopically placed clips are discovered.
A large hemlock clip from a previous cholecystectomy is visible. The surgeon places the entire gallbladder remnant into an endoscopic retrieval bag for removal.
The surgeon inspects the operative bed, ensuring hemostasis and confirming the absence of bile leakage. The common bile duct (CBD) is visibly intact, with no signs of bile leakage.
